title: Reading and Use of English - Part 5
description: You are going to read an article about renewable energy. For questions 31 – 36, choose the answer (A, B, C or D) which you think fits best according to the text. Mark your answers on the separate answer sheet.

content.title: The Power Shift
content.subtitle: An article about the growth and challenges of renewable energy.
content.text:
Something quietly extraordinary has happened to the global energy landscape in the past decade. Solar power, which was once considered too expensive to compete with fossil fuels without substantial government subsidies, is now in many parts of the world the cheapest source of electricity ever measured. [31] The cost of solar panels has fallen by more than ninety percent since 2010, a trajectory so steep that even the engineers and economists who advocated most strongly for renewable energy failed to predict it. [/31] The energy transition is happening faster than almost anyone expected — and yet, paradoxically, not fast enough.

The progress in wind energy has been equally striking. Offshore wind turbines have grown to heights that dwarf skyscrapers, their blades sweeping areas larger than a football pitch with each rotation. [32] In some northern European countries, wind power now regularly generates more electricity than total national demand on windy days, a situation that creates its own set of challenges — specifically, where to store the surplus. [/32]

Storage is the central unsolved problem of the renewable energy transition. Solar panels generate electricity when the sun shines; wind turbines generate it when the wind blows. Neither responds to demand. Grid-scale battery storage has improved dramatically, but current technology cannot yet store energy cheaply enough or for long enough to fully cover periods of low renewable generation lasting several days. [33] Engineers describe the challenge as 'the last mile' of the energy transition: the technology that would complete the system exists in principle, but has not yet been deployed at the necessary scale or cost. [/33]

The social and political dimensions of the transition are as complex as the technical ones. Renewable energy projects, despite their environmental benefits, frequently encounter strong local opposition. Wind farms are objected to on grounds of visual impact and noise. Solar farms occupy agricultural land. [34] Critics from within the environmental movement have pointed out the irony that the transition away from fossil fuels is itself generating a form of conflict over land and landscape, pitting ecological goals against the interests of local communities who bear the most visible consequences. [/34]

The workforce implications are also significant. The coal and gas industries, whose decline is accelerating, have historically employed communities in specific regions over generations, creating towns whose entire identity and economy were built around a single industry. [35] Retraining programmes, relocation support, and investment in new industries have been promised by governments across multiple transitions but delivered with uneven success. The workers most affected are rarely those who have benefited most from the economic growth that fossil fuels enabled. [/35]

None of this diminishes the scale of what has been achieved. Renewable energy is no longer an alternative technology on the margins of the energy system. [36] In less than a generation, it has moved from idealism to infrastructure — a shift as significant, in its own way, as the original transition from wood to coal that powered the Industrial Revolution. [/36] The question is no longer whether the transition will happen, but how equitably and how quickly.

questions.question: The fall in solar panel costs is described as particularly remarkable because
options: A) it was achieved without any government subsidies.
B) it exceeded even the most optimistic forecasts of renewable energy supporters.
C) it happened faster in developing countries than in Europe and North America.
D) it was accompanied by equally dramatic improvements in efficiency.
questions.explanation: Even 'the engineers and economists who advocated most strongly for renewable energy failed to predict' the trajectory, meaning the reality exceeded even optimistic forecasts.

questions.question: The situation in northern European countries where wind power exceeds national demand illustrates
options: A) the success of European energy policy in reducing dependence on fossil fuels.
B) that renewable energy has already solved the problem of energy security.
C) that high renewable capacity creates new problems as well as solving existing ones.
D) the need for better international cooperation on energy distribution.
questions.explanation: This situation 'creates its own set of challenges — specifically, where to store the surplus', showing that high renewable capacity generates new problems.

questions.question: The phrase 'the last mile' in the context of renewable energy refers to
options: A) the final stage of building a renewable energy installation.
B) the gap between current technology and what is needed to complete the transition.
C) the distance energy must travel from generation sites to consumers.
D) the remaining percentage of energy still produced by fossil fuels.
questions.explanation: Engineers describe the storage challenge as 'the last mile' — the technology 'exists in principle, but has not yet been deployed at the necessary scale or cost', meaning the gap between current and required capability.

questions.question: The irony noted by critics within the environmental movement is that
options: A) renewable energy is more expensive than fossil fuels for local communities.
B) the construction of renewable energy creates environmental damage.
C) the shift away from fossil fuels is creating new conflicts with local communities.
D) wind farms are being built in areas of outstanding natural beauty.
questions.explanation: The 'irony' is that the transition away from fossil fuels 'is itself generating a form of conflict over land and landscape', creating new tensions even as it addresses old ones.

questions.question: The writer's tone when discussing workers in coal and gas communities is
options: A) critical of governments who have failed to support these workers.
B) sympathetic, noting that these workers have been disproportionately affected.
C) optimistic about the effectiveness of retraining programmes.
D) indifferent, suggesting economic change inevitably produces disruption.
questions.explanation: The writer notes these workers 'are rarely those who have benefited most from the economic growth that fossil fuels enabled' — a sympathetic observation about an unfair burden.

questions.question: When the writer compares the energy transition to 'the original transition from wood to coal', they suggest that
options: A) the current transition will cause as much environmental damage as the Industrial Revolution.
B) the current transition is comparable in historical importance to a previous energy revolution.
C) renewable energy will generate the same kind of economic growth as fossil fuels did.
D) the transition to renewables has already been completed in the same way as the shift to coal.
questions.explanation: Describing it as 'a shift as significant, in its own way, as the original transition from wood to coal' places the current energy change on the same level of historical importance.
